query: extraction of disto-angular impacted mandibular third molar

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular third molars are the most frequently extracted teeth, accounting for 18% of dental extractions. [1] [2] Third molars typically develop around the age of 8 to 15 [3] and erupt between the ages of 17 to 22. [4] As a result of this delayed eruption, mandibular third molars are often impacted, with 17 to 69% presenting with some degree of impaction. [5] Extraction of third molars is further complicated by its anatomical proximity to the inferior alveolar nerve (IAN), the third branch of the fifth cranial nerve, which supplies sensory function to the lower cheek, chin, lip, tongue, gingivae, and teeth. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Mandibular Third Molar
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of impacted mandibular 3rd molars is a routine procedure in dental practice, as these teeth are most likely to develop some degree of impaction. The position of the mandibular 3rd molar serves as a key indicator of extraction complexity. Several classification systems, including Winter and Pell and Gregory, have been created to anticipate the difficulty of extracting the 3rd molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Impaction, proximity to the IAN, and restricted access all contribute to the increased complexity of mandibular third molar extraction. This article covers the anatomical considerations, assessment, indications, contraindications, and surgical techniques for extraction of lower third molars.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough examination of the lower third molar focuses on the position, accessibility, and degree of impaction to determine procedural complexity. The factors to be assessed are summarised in Table 1. These factors are assessed both clinically and radiographically. Note – Adapted from Gbotolorun et al. Assessment of Factors Associated With Surgical Difficulty in Impacted Mandibular Third Molar Extraction. J Oral Maxillofac Surg 2007. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indications for extraction of a lower third molar include recurrent episodes of pericoronitis, unrestorable caries, or caries extending into the pulp. Furthermore, a horizontal or mesioangular lower third molar causing disto-cervical caries in the lower second molar is indicated for extraction to facilitate restorative treatment in the lower second molar. In addition, extraction is also indicated in third molars with odontogenic cysts or tumors, those impeding surgery (such as in patients undergoing orthognathic surgery), or in the line of a mandibular fracture. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The envelope flap is one of the more commonly used for extracting impacted mandibular molars. This flap should extend from the 1st molar to the posterior aspect of the impacted tooth. The flap should also reflect laterally toward the external oblique ridge.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of one or more of these radiographic features indicates the need for further imaging by way of CBCT, which can provide further information on the relationship between the lower third molar and the IAN.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Indications -- Impacted Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: A joint decision should be had with the patient as to whether prophylactic extraction or radiographic monitoring is preferable. Impaction of teeth is seen most commonly with teeth late in eruption chronology - namely mandibular third molars, upper canines, and lower premolars. [19] National clinical guidelines often govern the specific indications for mandibular third molar removal.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth impaction may affect anyone, with incidence rates varying from 5.6% to 18.8%, and it tends to be more common in female individuals. The frequency of tooth impaction follows a pattern opposite to the typical chronological eruption sequence. Teeth that erupt later are more likely to be impacted due to insufficient arch space. The mandibular 3rd molar is the most commonly impacted tooth, followed by the maxillary 3rd molar, maxillary canine, mandibular premolars, maxillary premolars, and 2nd molar. The teeth with the lowest impaction rates are the mandibular incisors, 1st molars, and primary teeth. Impaction of these teeth is relatively rare and is typically associated with specific conditions, such as retained deciduous teeth or abnormalities like an odontoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Preparation -- Winter's Lines (WAR)
retrieved_docs.content: A formal index of quantification of surgical complexity of extraction of lower third molars was suggested by Winter in 1926. [15] This method involves the use of three illusory lines. The first of these is a white line that is drawn across the occlusal surfaces of the lower molars and extends posteriorly above the unerupted third molar. The second line, amber, follows the bone level from the distal aspect of the lower third molar, extending to the interseptal bone of the first molar. The third line, red, is a line drawn perpendicular from the white line extending down to the anticipated point of application on the unerupted lower third molar. This is usually the mesial aspect of the CEJ, except for disto-angular third molars, where the application point is distal. (See figure 2.)

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lower third molars are the most frequently extracted teeth, accounting for 18% of dental extractions. [1] [2] This is due to their posterior position intra-orally, coupled with the high likelihood of impaction, often resulting in pericoronitis. Pericoronitis is the inflammation of the soft tissues surrounding the crown of a partially erupted tooth. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lower anterior crowding is not an indication of extraction of lower third molars. Crowding of the lower anterior teeth is sometimes observed simultaneously with the eruption of lower wisdom teeth. This has led to the belief that the erupting third molars cause a mesial migration of teeth, consequently moving the incisors, resulting in crowding. [11] Several studies have investigated this hypothesis. Still, most have not found a cause-effect relationship between anterior crowding and the eruption of lower third molars. [11] Consequently, Zawawi & Melis (2014) argue that extraction of lower third molars does not alleviate anterior crowding and therefore does not justify the potential risk of damage to IAN. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pell and Gregory classification
retrieved_docs.content: The classification by Pell and Gregory describes impacted mandibular 3rd molars on the vertical and horizontal planes. The vertical relation of the impacted 3rd molar to the occlusal plane of the mandibular 2nd molar is classified as levels A, B, and C. The horizontal relation of the impacted molar with the anterior border of the mandibular ramus is classified as classes 1, 2, and 3. [6] The Pell and Gregory classification is as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications of extraction of lower third molars teeth are high risk of IAN damage, complex root morphology, hypercementosis, and ankylosis. In addition, third molars would not be extracted in patients with a history of intravenous bisphosphonate use or radiotherapy. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Loss of cortication of the inferior dental canal (IDC) - this is seen as an absence of the white 'tram lines' on plain film radiographs. Darkening of third molar roots - this is seen as a dark 'banding' across the apical area of the lower third molar roots. Deviation of the IDC - the IDC typically deviates away from the roots of the lower third molar. Narrowing of the IDC - this is characterized by localized narrowing of the IDC around the region of the lower third molar root. Dilaceration of roots - This is classified as excess curvature of the lower third molar roots. Narrowing of the third molar roots - This is seen as an abnormal narrowing of the apical area of the lower third molar roots.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.
